class.name: ResourceImporterTexture
class.description: This importer provides [Texture2D] resources for use in the project. [b]Supported image formats[/b] [codeblock] - BMP (.bmp) - No support for 16-bit per pixel images. Only 1-bit, 4-bit, 8-bit, 24-bit, and 32-bit per pixel images are supported. DirectDraw Surface (.dds) - If mipmaps are present in the texture, they will be loaded directly. This can be used to achieve effects using custom mipmaps. - OpenEXR (.exr) - Supports HDR (highly recommended for panorama skies). - Radiance HDR (.hdr) - Supports HDR (highly recommended for panorama skies). - JPEG (.jpg, .jpeg) - Doesn't support transparency per the format's limitations. - PNG (.png) - Precision is limited to 8 bits per channel upon importing (no HDR images). - Truevision Targa (.tga) - SVG (.svg, .svgz) - SVGs are rasterized using NanoSVG when importing them. Support is limited; complex vectors may not render correctly. For complex vectors, rendering them to PNGs using Inkscape is often a better solution. This can be automated thanks to its command-line interface. - WebP (.webp) [/codeblock] The resulting [Texture2D] is saved into a [code].stex[/code] file, which is a Godot-specific texture format.

members.member: The lossy compression quality to use. Higher values result in higher quality, at the cost of larger file sizes and slower encoding speeds. This option affects both [b]Lossy (WebP)[/b] and [b]VRAM (S3TC/ETC/BPTC)[/b] compression.
If [code]true[/code], generates mipmaps for use by the 2D and 3D renderer. [b]Note:[/b] Regardless of this option's value, custom mipmaps are preserved when importing a DDS file.
The maximum number of mipmaps to generate. This can

If [code]true[/code], inverts the texture's green channel when importing the texture. This can be used to invert a normal map's Y direction. Godot expects normal maps to use X+, Y-, and Z+ coordinates. If your normal map uses a Y+ coordinate (sometimes called "DirectX format"), you may have to enable this option. See [url=http://wiki.polycount.com/wiki/Normal_Map_Technical_Details#Common_Swizzle_Coordinates]this page[/url] for a comparison of normal map coordinates expected by popular engines.

If set to a value greater than [code]0[/code], limits the size of the texture's largest dimension while preserving the aspect ratio. This can be used to keep source high-resolution assets while importing lower-resolution textures.

The color channel to use as the roughness map. If in doubt, set this to [b]Green[/b] as it will work for both grayscale and ORM maps (see also [ORMMaterial3D]). When following the ORM standard, the roughness map is stored in the texture's green channel. This option works in tandem with [member roughness/src_normal]. [b]Note:[/b] Only relevant in 3D when using a texture as a roughness map for physically-based rendering.

The normal map to use as a reference to limit the roughness map intensity. This reduces specular aliasing slightly with no run-time performance cost. This option works in tandem with [member roughness/mode]. [b]Note:[/b] Only relevant in 3D when using a texture as a roughness map for physically-based rendering.

The scale to use when rasterizing a SVG file to a bitmap image. [code]1.0[/code] matches the original scale defined in the SVG file. Higher values result in a larger texture, which can look crisper on higher resolution displays. [b]Note:[/b] Since this affects the texture's size in pixels, the project will have to be adapted to display the image at the expected physical size (e.g. using [member Node2D.scale] or [member Camera2D.zoom]). [b]Note:[/b] Godot always rasterizes SVG files when importing them. Rendering SVG files as vectors or polygons isn't supported yet.
